name: water_treatment_uv_sensor_blindspot
premise: Renee Alvarez, superintendent of the Brookmere water treatment plant, must decide whether to issue a boil-water advisory after a sensor discrepancy appears in the ultraviolet disinfection system. A new control algorithm has reduced chemical use by 24% and prevented taste complaints that previously caused residents to switch to unsafe private wells. Engineer Pavel Sokolov finds that during high turbidity spikes after storms, the UV sensor array can overstate disinfection effectiveness if one downstream sensor is fouled. Rain is forecast overnight, but issuing an advisory now will close schools, disrupt dialysis clinics, and cause panic in a city still recovering from a previous water crisis. Waiting for confirmatory lab results avoids unnecessary disruption but may expose residents to untreated pathogens if the blindspot is real.

agents.name: Renee Alvarez
agents.identity: I am the water plant superintendent responsible for public health, regulatory compliance, and community trust.
agents.goal: I want to avoid exposing residents to unsafe water while preventing unnecessary panic and service disruption.

agents.name: Pavel Sokolov
agents.identity: I am the treatment systems engineer who discovered the UV sensor blindspot during storm-readiness testing.
agents.goal: I want the city to act before a possible disinfection failure reaches households.

choices: I will issue a precautionary boil-water advisory immediately, accepting citywide disruption before confirmatory lab results arrive.
I will wait for lab confirmation while increasing sampling and sensor cleaning overnight, accepting possible exposure during the delay.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Renee Alvarez must decide whether to warn the city now or wait for confirmation under storm conditions. What does Renee Alvarez do?
